Raphinha hit a hat-trick as Barcelona thrashed Sevilla 5-2 to restore their four-point lead at the top of La Liga. After Real Madrid cut the gap by beating Elche on Saturday, the Spanish champions responded by romping to a comfortable victory.

Dani Olmo and João Cancelo also struck for Hansi Flick’s side, who host Newcastle in the Champions League on Wednesday. Fans streamed to the stadium to vote in the club’s presidential elections, with either Joan Laporta or Victor Font to be announced the new chief later on Sunday night.

After the 1-1 draw in Newcastle in the last-16 first leg, Flick left Lamine Yamal on the bench and handed the 18-year-old Xavi Espart his first start, at right-back. Barcelona were handed two penalties by a sorry Sevilla side to get off to the perfect start at the partially rebuilt Camp Nou, which finally received clearance to open the north stand.

About 56,000 fans attended and they were able to watch a fairly straightforward rout of the Andalucían side. Djibril Sow clumsily clipped a rampaging Cancelo in the box and Raphinha dinked home from the spot to open the scoring in the ninth minute.

The Brazilian winger was afforded another penalty to put Barça two goals up when José Ángel Carmona, lying on the floor, handled as Cancelo tried to dribble around him. This time Raphinha fired it low to the left and although Odysseas Vlachodimos went the right way he could not keep it out.

The hosts scored a third in the 38th minute when Marc Bernal’s deflected cross from the left found Olmo, who produced a clinical finish. Sevilla pulled one back before the break when Juanlu Sánchez got in behind Cancelo and crossed for Oso to score unmarked at the back post.

Raphinha completed his hat-trick early in the second half with a heavily deflected shot as Barça strolled in the spring sunshine. Cancelo capped an impressive attacking display with a goal of his own, driving into the box and finishing through Vlachodimos’s legs.

Flick sent on the 21-year-old midfielder Gavi for the final 10 minutes, for his first appearance since August after suffering a severe knee injury. Sow pulled another one back for Sevilla in stoppage time, nodding home Oso’s cross, with Barcelona already thinking about Newcastle’s visit on Wednesday.

Milan fell to a 1-0 defeat at Lazio on Sunday after Gustav Isaksen struck in the first half, leaving the visitors eight points adrift of the Serie A leaders, Inter.

View image in fullscreen Gustav Isaksen slots home Lazio’s winner past Mike Maignan. Photograph: Andrew Medichini/AP

Milan had the chance to close the gap on their city rivals after Inter were held to a 1-1 home draw by Atalanta on Saturday but failed to capitalise in a match in which they struggled to assert control.

Lazio made the breakthrough in the 26th minute when Isaksen broke free from his marker to chase a long ball down the wing before firing home from a tight angle. Milan pushed forward in the second half and created several chances but could not find an equaliser.

The defeat left Milan on 60 points, with Inter on 68 with nine matches remaining. Lazio are ninth with 40 points.

Como remained in the hunt for a first appearance in the Champions League after beating 10-man Roma 2-1 in Serie A.

Trailing at the break to Donyell Malen’s early penalty, Como showed why they have shaken things up in Italy’s top flight by coming back to beat key rivals for the final spot in Europe’s elite club competition.

Cesc Fàbregas’s small but wealthy team are fourth, a point above Juventus thanks to Tasos Douvikas’s low drive in the 59th minute and Diego Carlos – who gave away Roma’s penalty – poking in the winner in front of delighted home fans 20 minutes later.

Between those two goals the Roma defender Wesley was sent off for two bookable offences as the away side failed to deal with an onslaught in the second half.

Como have never played European football in their history but there is a real chance that Champions League football might be played at the tiny Stadio Giuseppe Sinigaglia, in view of one of the world’s most famous lakes.

View image in fullscreen Diego Carlos is congratulated by Como teammates after scoring the winner against Roma. Photograph: Image Photo Agency/Getty Images

The substitute Vitinha and Leo Ostigard scored in Genoa’s 2-0 win at bottom-place Verona as Daniele De Rossi’s side moved even further away from the relegation zone.

Genoa are 13th with 33 points after 29 rounds, nine points above 18th-place Cremonese.

Pisa claimed only their second league win of the season despite being reduced to 10 men in the 37th minute against Cagliari when Rafiu Durosinmi was sent off. Antonio Caracciolo scored twice in a 3-1 victory with Cagliari also finishing with 10 men following Adam Obert’s late dismissal for a second yellow card.

Bologna beat Sassuolo 1-0, with Thijs Dallinga scoring early in the game.

Deniz Undav scored the only goal in Stuttgart’s 1-0 home win over RB Leipzig in the Bundesliga, sending the fourth-placed hosts three points clear of the visitors.

Undav pounced on an error from the goalkeeper Maarten Vandevoordt to score his 16th goal of the season and move Stuttgart level with third-placed Hoffenheim on 50 points.

The best chance of the first half fell to Christoph Baumgartner, Leipzig’s top scorer this season, but the Austrian blasted well over the bar when one-on-one with home goalkeeper Alexander Nübel.

With 56 minutes gone, Vandevoordt passed out directly to Stuttgart’s Chris Führich, who squared for Undav to slide home and score for a fifth straight game.

Leipzig’s Willi Orban headed against the inside of the post in stoppage time but Stuttgart held on for a crucial victory.

Jeong Woo-yeong scored in stoppage time and Matheo Raab produced a spectacular late save on his Union Berlin debut in a 1-0 win at Freiburg. Union’s first away win of 2026 took them seven points clear of the relegation playoff spot.

Woo-yeong curled a stunner through the crowded penalty box before Raab leapt acrobatically to keep out a last-ditch Jan-Niklas Beste effort in the 12th minute of stoppage time.

Mainz won 2-0 at Werder Bremen to overtake the hosts and move three points clear of the relegation playoff spot.

Bremen’s woes were summed up when their defender Marco Grüll seemed to be distracted by a pigeon walking on the field as Mainz took a quick throw for Paul Nebel’s fifth-minute opener. Lee Jae-Sung added another in the second half.

Lyon’s bid for automatic Champions League qualification suffered another blow in a 0-0 draw at 10-man Le Havre in Ligue 1. Paulo Fonseca’s men have gone four league games without a win to slip from the fringes of the title race to fourth, two points behind Marseille.

Le Havre had Stephan Zagadou sent off 10 minutes in to the second half but Lyon mustered just one shot on target in a tame display and are now only four points clear of fifth-placed Monaco and sixth-placed Rennes.

Gary O’Neil’s Strasbourg fell six points off the European places after a goalless stalemate with Paris FC.

Toulouse ended a six-match winless run in the league as Mario Sauer scored a dramatic 99th-minute winner in a 4-3 victory at the bottom club, Metz.

Football.title: Ashley Cole lands first managerial role as head coach of Serie B side Cesena
Football.content:
Ashley Cole has been appointed as the head coach of the Serie B side Cesena. It is a first management role for the former Arsenal, Chelsea and England full-back, who has been developing as a coach since retiring from playing in 2019.

Cole’s positions have included working with Frank Lampard at Derby, Chelsea and Everton, with Wayne Rooney at Birmingham, and with England and the Football Association.

Now the 45-year-old has made an unexpected move to Italy’s second tier, taking over at Cesena, who are eighth in the table and sacked Michele Mignani on Saturday.

Cole has signed a short-term deal until the end of June but with the option to extend “upon the fulfilment of certain conditions”.

A statement on the Cesena website read: “The entire Cesena FC family is thrilled to welcome manager Ashley Cole to lead the Bianconeri first team and extends a warm welcome to him and his staff in Cesena, wishing them all the best of luck in this new adventure.”

Cole will be assisted by the former Chelsea academy coach Jack Mesure.

Football.title: Leeds keep on track for survival despite Gudmundsson red card against Palace
Football.content:
Having moved to within touching distance of Premier League safety when they beat Nottingham Forest last month, three points from their past five games could be raising a few jitters among the Leeds supporters. But they were celebrating with Daniel Farke and his players at full time after earning a point that the German predicted could turn out to be “priceless” in the end.

Had Dominic Calvert-Lewin converted from the spot in the first half after Will Hughes gifted them a penalty the celebrations in the away end would have been even louder. Up until that point, Leeds had been the much better side as Crystal Palace again struggled against their physical approach.

However, the sending off of Gabriel Gudmundsson after a comical moment when the referee, Thomas Bramall, forgot that the Sweden defender had already been cautioned, changed the complexion of the game. A toothless Palace still could not make their advantage count as they were held to a stalemate at home for the second time in four days and left the field to a few boos again.

Leeds dug in and deservedly emerged with a share of the points, even if Brennan Johnson – who was lucky to escape being shown a red card of his own – was offside by a matter of millimetres in setting up Jefferson Lerma.

“It was another day when I trust these players with my life because I can always count on them,” said a delighted Farke, who also described them as “heroes”.

Oliver Glasner prioritised Palace’s trip to Elland Road in December over their Conference League commitments but his first-choice side were promptly thumped 4-1. This time with the trip to Cyprus in the second leg of their last-16 tie on Thursday in mind, he gave Adam Wharton and Daichi Kamada a rest and handed Hughes the captain’s armband in the absence of Dean Henderson.

That meant a first Premier League start for Walter Benítez, whose last appearance came in the infamous FA Cup defeat by non-league Macclesfield, and the Argentina goalkeeper had a busy first half.

While Evann Guessand almost made Karl Darlow pay for dawdling over a back pass in the third minute, the tone for an ill-tempered game was set when Pascal Struijk hacked down the Ivory Coast forward soon after. Brendan Aaronson directed his shot just wide after Ismaila Sarr misjudged a header before Calvert-Lewin saw two headers from corners saved by Benítez, with replays showing that the majority of the ball had crossed the line on the first. By contrast, Palace looked lethargic after their exertions in midweek and created largely nothing despite Guessand’s best efforts.

The real drama was reserved for injury time in the first half. Leeds were awarded a penalty when Hughes inexplicably handled inside the area but Calvert-Lewin could only drag his effort well wide of the target. Then, after Jake Bijol had escaped a second caution for bringing down Sarr and both Glasner and Farke were shown yellow cards in the ensuing melee, Gudmundsson took out the Senegal forward a minute later. Bramall booked him before he eventually realised his mistake after being reminded by Sarr. The Palace defender Jaydee Canvot pumped his fist in delight when the red card finally came out.

View image in fullscreen Anguish for Dominic Calvert-Lewin after missing a penalty. Photograph: Glyn Kirk/AFP/Getty Images

“Within two minutes, it seems like the whole world is against you,” admitted Farke, who felt the challenge didn’t warrant a caution. “But it was another proof why when this club is united and you show this mentality, then we can overcome all adversity.”

Given how they have struggled against a low block in the Conference League, Canvot should have known that playing against 10 men would be no guarantee of a Palace victory. They continued to look devoid of ideas as Leeds sat in their shape and waited for the mistakes that came regularly.

Glasner’s decision to withdraw the ineffective Jørgen Strand Larsen for Jean-Philippe Mateta was again greeted with boos, although perhaps mainly due to its negativity this time rather than any lingering grievances against the France striker.

“We are not the biggest goalscoring machine in the Premier League. It is an issue, we know,” said Glasner. “But to me it feels [we are] a little bit nervous. We want to score because we are one player up and we are taking bad decisions and losing our structure. We have to stay calm and wait for the right moments.”

The change at least gave Palace new impetus as Mateta forced Darlow into a full-stretch save after Sarr’s cross was diverted off his chest before – to Farke’s relief – Struijk’s headed clearance shaved his own crossbar. Johnson got away with a second yellow card for tripping James Justin and the Leeds fans made their feelings clear to Bramall. They had a point.

Palace looked as if they could have played for another two hours and still not found a way past Darlow. Glasner must find a way of inspiring his players going into another long week.

News.title: Desperate Iran begs for DONATIONS to keep up its sickening reign of terror across Middle East
News.content:
IRAN’S flailing regime is begging for donations to prop up funding for its reign of terror in the Middle East.

Desperate officials have resorted to sharing bank details on social media – hoping to claw in handouts from “benevolent friends”.





Staff at Iran‘s embassy in India have even called on “our dear brothers and sisters” to bring cash donations straight to them.

It comes as Iran’s battered regime fights for survival as blitzes by the US and Israel continue to wipe out top leaders and key infrastructure.

Military chiefs running Iran’s war machine are pumping everything they can into retaliating with repeated missile and drone strikes on Gulf nations housing US bases.

Iran launched thousands of drones at targets across the Middle East within days of Donald Trump launching Epic Fury in a scrambling attempt to get the upper hand by overwhelming defences.



But after more than two weeks of trading blows, Iran’s surviving leaders are frantically trying to keep funding its war efforts.

In a twisted post, Iran’s embassy in New Delhi pleaded with “charitable and benevolent members” to send donations.

“We would like to express our sincere gratitude to our dear Indian brothers and sisters for their continued support,” the embassy said.

It comes as Trump could cut the murderous regime’s economic jugular by seizing Kharg Island – home to its crucial oil infrastructure.



Exports from the tiny base – where 90 per cent of Iran’s oil is stored and shipped from – make up around 40 per cent of Iran’s budget.

Wrestling control from Iran’s bloodthirsty mullahs would choke their ability to fund weapons wreaking havoc across the Middle East.

Trump unleashed strikes on military targets on the island on Friday night – leaving critical oil infrastructure intact in a warning to the regime.

The president warned the oil sites would be next in the firing “should Iran, or anyone else” continue to disrupt shipping in the Strait of Hormuz.

But Trump could be looking to checkmate Iran by seizing control of the island and using it as a bargaining chip, rather than obliterating it.



Navy Seals, assault submarines and stealth helicopters will be waiting in the wings to launch a raid on the five-mile lifeline island.

Meanwhile, Trump has demanded seven countries send warships to keep the Strait of Hormuz.

He said the US is negotiating with countries heavily reliant on Middle East crude to join a coalition to police the vital waterway where around a fifth of the world’s traded oil normally flows.



The Iranian regime’s desperate measures come as the Islamic Republic reels from the loss of dozens of its top clerics – including Ayatollah Ali Khamenei.

Turmoil has consumed the rogue nation, with Khamenei’s son Mojtaba anointed as the Supreme Leader just days ago.

The new chief mullah, 56, has not been seen since his evil father and family members were killed in the initial strikes of operation Epic Fury, however.

Mojtaba – who is being used a figurehead for the war – is understood to have been maimed in the same airstrike that killed his father and obliterated his compound in Tehran.

He has now been put on a ventilator in a hospital’s intensive care unit.



Sources in Iran told The Sun today he has been put on a life support machine and is now unable to breathe unattended.

An insider with links to the hospital treating him revealed last week that he was in a coma, had lost at least one leg and suffered possible liver and stomach wounds.

The same source has today revealed that the fanatical Islamist firebrand’s condition has worsened and he is now being kept alive by machines.



Mojtaba is not thought to have had any day-to-day control over the war – with the Islamic Revolutionary Guards Corp spearheading the ruthless campaign instead.

An Israeli government source told The Sun: “It is absolutely irrefutable that Mojtaba Khamenei has been wounded.

“But his condition actually has little bearing on the course of the war as he is a puppet put in place by the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps.

“They hold the power now and they are running Iran.”

It comes as the collapsing regime ramps up terror on the streets as it fears an uprising from its repressed population.



Sources in Iran told The Sun how a barbaric crackdown involving armed patrols, brutal checkpoints and mass arrests has been unleashed.

One person inside Tehran said: “Since the bombings began, the regime’s forces have been patrolling the streets fully armed out of sheer fear.

“They know that if even a small opening is created, the people will rise up again, and they are terrified of the masses pouring into the streets.”

Iranians have been under the repressive thumb of the regime since the Islamic Revolution toppled the Shah dynasty in 1979.

But after the US and Israel began blitzing the country on February 28, Trump called on the population to seize the opportunity to oust it for good.



Flames of rebellion have been fanned as missiles and drones continue to rain down – with the memory of January’s deadly protests still fresh in the minds of millions of Iranians yearning for the end of the regime’s rule.

In the capital, people stopped at checkpoints are having their mobile phones snatched from their hands to be checked.

Ruthless militants search to see whether the owner has communicated with a number outside the country – and if they have, they are arrested, insiders say.

In some areas, individuals are stopped at checkpoints up to six times a day.

RECOMMENDED STORIES

One source in the city of Rasht told how at checkpoints, teenagers as young as 14 have been armed with weapons.



The baby-faced members of Basij – a paramilitary volunteer militia within the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps – have been ordered to confiscate phones and scrutinise activity.

News.title: Is this the world’s first robot ARREST? Bizarre moment humanoid is detained by police after ‘harassing elderly woman’
News.content:
THIS is the moment a humanoid robot was arrested after harassing an elderly woman forcing cops to intervene.

A humanoid robot was taken into custody by Macau police after it appeared to harass an elderly woman on a public street.





The video shows the robot being surrounded by people on the street as the nearly 70-year-old woman berated it.

Filmed by an onlooker, the robot was seen raising its arms twice as the woman yelled at it, clearly frustrated.

“You’re making my heart race,” she told the robot in Cantonese.



“You can do plenty but you want to cause this mess? You’re crazy.”

The woman – wearing a blue shirt and white pants – pointed her finger at the robot as others watched on in disbelief.

Police were later seen escorting the robot away from the scene.

Macau is a special administrative region of China.



According to local media, the woman was later taken to hospital.

She was not physically injured in the strange ordeal and was later discharged.

Cops say she had been on her phone when the robot suddenly appeared behind her.

It has since been revealed that the machine belonged to an education centre.

The centre had been using the new technology for promotional activities.



It was returned to its 50-year-old operator nearby.

The operator said the robot had been supervised remotely, however was also guided by mixed programming.

The scary incident happened on a narrow street when the elderly woman stopped to check her phone.

The robot waited ominously behind her.

The owner admitted the woman was likely startled when she turned around to see the machine.



He has since apologised and has been given a warning from authorities.

Social media users have weighed in on the shocking footage.

Some joked about the humanoid machine being arrested by police.

Others said the woman overreacted.

It has been alleged that the android in the video was a G1 – unveiled in May 2024 by Chinese company Unitree.



It is one of the more “affordable” robots available on the market – costing an estimated £10,177 ($13,500) to make.

Standing 130cm tall and weighing around 77 pounds, the robot has a 360-degree detection perception camera attached to its face and a speaker on its chest.

The Hangzhou-based company said the G1 has more flexibility than the average human body.

This is due to its 23-43 joint motors from head to toe.

The company website said the robot “is sensitive and reliable, and can simulate human hands to achieve precise operation of objects.”



Its battery life is around two hours, running up to two metres a second, and is evolving on a daily basis with the help of artificial intelligence.

Robots have begun to appear on more streets around the world over the last 12 months, from small machines on wheels delivering food to humanoid machines roaming the streets.

Last September, a Unitree G1 robot was spotted roaming the streets in Brisbane, Australia.

John Roberts, Professor in Robotics at Queensland University of Technology (QUT), said it had been purchased for its robotics department so students could get a “basic understanding” of robots and what they can do now versus in the future.

Speaking to the Courier Mail, he said: “we have had this robot for about four weeks now and we are getting to grips with how it works and starting to plan how we will direct our robotics research to take advantage of these kinds of general-purpose robots – humanoids.”



“Our robot has basic capabilities at the moment, it can walk, it can run, it can stand,” he said.

RECOMMENDED STORIES

“We want our students to get a basic understanding of what a humanoid robot can and can’t do at the moment and what they might be able to do with some more development.

“The focus of our research is on how these robots will be used in the future and then what needs to be done to get to that point.”

News.title: Paranoid Putin massively steps up Moscow security clampdown with armed officers flooding streets and internet blackout
News.content:
VLADIMIR Putin has massively stepped up security in Moscow with an internet blackout and armed officers flooding the streets.

The tyrant, 73, has ordered a security clampdown at the Kremlin amid fears his safety has been compromised in the same way that led to the killing of Iran’s supreme leader Ali Khamenei.





Paranoia has engulfed the Russian dictator’s security apparatus amid fears his vast electronic surveillance machinery is riddled by vulnerabilities allowing his movements to be monitored, say reports.

This could lead to an assassination bid on the 73-year-old Kremlin ruler, it is feared.

New images show armed Federal Security Officers and vehicles armed with electronic warfare equipment to suppress drones around the Kremlin amid the new clampdown.



Officers are even visibly posted on Lenin’s mausoleum on Red Square, as revealed by channel VChK-OGPU with connections to the security services.

The paranoia is believed to be behind a massive blackout of mobile internet in Moscow and elsewhere in Russia, plunging the country back into the pre-web dark ages.

This unexplained act has caused mayhem with businesses closing, and taxi drivers and couriers unable to find their way around the city.

Ukraine has stepped up drone attacks on Putin’s capital in recent days, including last night, but the fear gripping his security chiefs relates to technological holes in the apparatus of his surveillance state.



“This is connected with Iran, with what America and Israel are doing in Iran,” expert commentator Vyacheslav Shiryaev told independent Republic journal.

“Everything happening in Iran is tied to hacking infrastructure that is supposed to monitor citizens, but it turns out that this infrastructure helps destroy heads of state instead…

“It is known that Israeli intelligence gained access to surveillance cameras in Tehran.

“That is precisely why the Kremlin is now tearing through the entire IT infrastructure, trying to understand where the vulnerability may be, how control over Putin’s movements, his motorcades and everything else could be carried out through the very infrastructure they themselves built in Moscow in order to monitor citizens.”

The internet blackout was ordered by Putin’s own security service, the FSO, he said.



In the wake of Khamenei’s killing on February 28, “something went wrong” for the FSO – Federal Protective Service – and “they saw the vulnerabilities”.

“Clearly they do not understand how to close those vulnerabilities without breaking everything they themselves need.”

The surveillance and face recognition technology used by Moscow and Tehran was similar.



Putin’s security chiefs realised the vulnerabilities “touched on the security of the first person [Putin] – then suddenly everybody started moving”.

Shiryaev predicted serious economic damage for the already-struggling Russian economy, enmeshed in a draining war with Ukraine.

“The authorities are walking through a swamp, sinking, but still moving on and getting stuck even deeper,” he said.

“This is undermining the foundations of the [Putin] regime, and resources are dwindling.”

The reason behind the internet blackout was “not Ukrainian drones and not cyberattacks, but the ruling elite’s fear for its own safety,” said Republic.



The Kremlin has been vague on the switch off which has led to soaring sales of paper maps and Russians even trying to use old-style pagers.

Sales of pagers, walkie-talkies and landline phones have skyrocketed while sales of paper maps have almost tripled.

Putin’s spokesman linked it to draconian new laws signed recently by the Kremlin dictator.

It was “related to the most important need—ensuring security”, he said, without going into detail.

“As for Putin, I do not think he appears in Moscow at all” currently due to the acute fears, said Shiryaev.



“Nobody knows where he really is.”

The siege in Moscow is particularly focused around key security and military facilities suspected of conniving in an extraordinary plot to oust the dictator.

The black out comes amid circulating rumours of a power-challenge by Putin’s most senior security council official Sergei Shoigu, an unconfirmed theory first aired by VChK-OGPU.

Dozens of Ukrainian drones were launched at Moscow for the third day in succession, forcing a response from the city’s air defences.

RECOMMENDED STORIES

Ukraine also ignited in flames the Labinsk oil refinery in Russia’s Krasnodar region.



Russia was today targeting the Kyiv region with missiles after strikes on civilians in Zaporizhzhia.

News.title: UK working on ‘viable’ plan to reopen Strait of Hormuz – as Keir says US attacks have ‘massively weakened Iran regime’
News.content:
Sir Keir said the Government was working with partners to find a coordinated response

HORMUZ CRISIS UK working on ‘viable’ plan to reopen Strait of Hormuz – as Keir says US attacks have ‘massively weakened Iran regime’

SIR Keir Starmer today vowed to work with allies on a “viable collective plan” to reopen the Strait of Hormuz as the war with Iran threatens to hammer global oil supplies.

The Prime Minister said restoring shipping through the crucial Gulf waterway was now a top international priority as energy markets brace for further turmoil.





But he stopped short of committing British warships to any mission despite US President Donald Trump urging allies to join a naval effort to secure the strait.

Speaking at a Downing Street press conference, Sir Keir said the Government was working with partners to find a coordinated response.

He warned that reopening the shipping lane – through which roughly a fifth of the world’s oil passes – would not be straightforward.

Sir Keir said it was clear the US operation “has massively weakened the military capability of the abhorrent regime in Iran”.

But added: “Ultimately, we have to reopen the Strait of Hormuz to ensure stability in the market…

“We are working with all of our allies, including our European partners, to bring together a viable collective plan that can restore freedom of navigation in the region as quickly as possible.”

He declined to spell out what specific role the UK could play if such a plan is agreed.

Sir Keir told reporters ministers were “looking at options” and assessing the capabilities Britain could contribute.



He added that the UK already had autonomous mine-hunting systems in the region, which are designed to clear explosives from shipping lanes.

But he stressed any decision on further military involvement would be taken carefully and alongside allies.

Setting out his approach to the conflict, Sir Keir said Britain had three priorities.

He said: “First, we will protect our people in the region.

“Second, while taking the necessary action to defend ourselves and our allies, we will not be drawn into the wider war.



“And third, we will keep working towards a swift resolution that brings security and stability back to the region and stops the Iranian threat to its neighbours.”

News.title: Iran’s missing new Supreme Leader is on life support after ‘being maimed in airstrike that killed his terrorist father’
News.content:
IRAN’s new leader has been put on a life support machine and is now unable to breathe unaided, sources in the country told The Sun today.

Mojtaba Khamenei – anointed Supreme leader of the rogue state just eight days ago – was maimed by an airstrike and has now been put on a ventilator in a hospital’s intensive care unit.





But the military chiefs running Iran’s war machine are continuing to launch drones and missiles across the war torn region, while using him as a figurehead.

A Sun source with links to the hospital treating 56-year-old Khamenei revealed last week that he was in a coma, had lost at least one leg and suffered possible liver and stomach wounds.

And the same source has today revealed that the fanatical Islamist firebrand’s condition has worsened and he is now being kept alive by machines.

The source said: “His brain is in a very low state of consciousness and his heart and lungs are only functioning because they are connected to a life support machine.

“He would be dead without the ventilator.”

Khamenei is believed to have been critically wounded in the same strike which killed his Supreme Leader father and Ayatollah Ali, 86, as well as Mojtaba’s wife and eight year-old son.

Multiple sources have since claimed he is not in day-to-day control and Iran’s Islamic Revolutionary Guards Corp is running the war wrecking the West’s economies.



He has not been seen since his election – and has only issued one rant blasting his enemies which was read out by a female state news TV anchor.

It comes as…

Khamenei Snr created an emergency power structure which handed control of the nation’s military to 31 separate regional IRGC commands if the leadership was wiped out.

And Iran’s war machine is now effectively on autopilot with no end in sight to the conflict detonated by US president Donald Trump and Israel on February 28.

The TV statement purported to come from Mojtaba vowed bloody revenge on his enemies over the death of his slain father and Iran’s “martyrs”, pledging “rivers would run with blood”.



But it was far from clear last night whether the wounded leader had any involvement in the lengthy blast aired in his name.

The statement pledged “we will be victorious” and repeated claims that Iran was only targeting American bases in surrounding countries.



It read: “We send a message to the leaders of the region and emphasise that we are going to have good relations with the countries around us.

“But the existence of the US bases in some of these countries and usage of those bases to attack Iran is not benefiting the region and they must be closed.

“As we said, we are not an enemy of the countries around us, and we are only targeting the bases of those Americans.

“The cleverness of our people has surprised the enemy.”

Khamenei’s statement also vowed to keep the vital Strait of Hormuz oil waterway between Iran and oil rich states would be kept shut.



Follow The Sun’s liveblog for the latest updates on Iran

Israeli and US officials are now convinced that Mojtaba is incapacitated and that the rogue state is being run by hardline Islamist fanatics true to his late father’s twisted creed.

And fears were growing that the drone and missile-firing militias had been primed and readied to fight on indefinitely as oil price soared and markets wobbled.

Iranian state TV now openly refers to the late ayatollah’s successor as “Jaanbaz of Ramadan” – which means wounded war veteran – amid concerns he has become a “ghost ayatollah.”

Security remained tight yesterday at Tehran’s Sina University Hospital in the city’s historic quarter after a section of the building was sealed off surrounded by guards.



The source – who asked not to be identified fearing for his life – managed to dodge Iran’s near total internet blackout to send messages to an exiled dissident based in London.

Members of the hospital’s trauma team told the Sun source Mojtaba was in “very serious” condition under the care of Mohammad Reza Zafargjani.

Zafargjani is Iran’s Minister of health, Treatment and Medical Education but also one of the country’s top trauma surgeons.

Veteran surgeon Zafargjani, 70, was said to have been assisted by Dr. Mohammad Marashi, a senior surgeon at Shahid Beheshti University of Medical Sciences.

The Sun source said in his message: “One or two of his legs have been cut off. His liver or stomach has also ruptured. He is apparently in a coma as well.”



Zafargjani’s Persian Wikipedia page reveals he honed his skills after eight years operating in the bloody Iran-Iraq War in the 1980’s.

Iranian President Masoud Pezeshkian was also reported to have visited the Sina Hospital last week accompanied by Dr Zafarghandi.

Other sources reported alleged Mojtada is entirely unaware of the ongoing war, the deaths of family members including his wife and son – and even his own election as Supreme Leader.



Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei put his nation’s was wiped out in the first action of Operation Epic Fury.

Mojtaba succeeded him on May 8 but has never given a public speech and has stayed silent amid rumours he is already dead.

The fate of Mojtaba – who lost his wife, Zahra, eight-year-old son Bagher as well as his mother and sister in the blast which killed his 86-year-old father – remained unclear last night.

Khamenei Snr is said to have watched and learned in 2003 when US forces conquered neighbour Iraq in 2003 by capturing and killing leader Saddam Hussein and his top lieutenants.

He divided Iran – which is seven times bigger than the UK – into 31 autonomous command centres of the all-powerful Islamic Revolutionary Guards Corps.



Iranian sources and analysts revealed yesterday that these self-governing commands are now running Iran’s war.

An Israeli government source told The Sun: “It is absolutely irrefutable that Mojtaba Khamenei has been wounded.

RECOMMENDED STORIES

“But his condition actually has little bearing on the course of the war as he is a puppet put in place by the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps.

“They hold the power now and they are running Iran.”

News.title: My ex-husband shot dead our daughter, 23, during row about Trump while on holiday… cops must reconsider charging him
News.content:
MUM'S TRAUMA My ex-husband shot dead our daughter, 23, during row about Trump while on holiday… cops must reconsider charging him

A MUM whose ex-husband shot his 23-year-old daughter dead after a row about Trump is asking cops to reconsider charging him.

Lucy Harrison, 23, was shot through the heart by her dad Kris Harrison, 52, while he was “showing off his gun” at his home in Texas in January last year.





Lucy’s mum, Jane Coates, 49, said US police failed to examine the recklessness of her ex-husband’s actions, his intoxication or his contradictions in court.

After American cops dropped their investigation without charging Kris a British coroner ruled his actions “reckless” and delivered an unlawful killing verdict to conclude an inquest into Lucy’s death.

Lucy was killed in the deeply Republican Prosper, Texas, while on holiday visiting her dad in the town – which is popular among British ex-pats.

She passed away on January 10, 2025, the same day her and her boyfriend were due to fly home to Warrington, Cheshire.

Cheshire Coroner Jacqueline Devonish concluded an inquest into her death last month.

The coroner said: “To shoot her through the chest whilst she was standing would have required him to have been pointing the gun without checking for bullets and pulled the trigger. I find this reckless.”

Lucy’s mum believes her daughter would want a full investigation into the tragedy.



Jane first found out about her daughter’s death when there was a knock at her door and the awful news was delivered.

“There’s been an accident, Lucy’s been shot and she’s not made it”, Jane was told by Lucy’s boyfriend’s mum.

It wasn’t until later that Jane found out that her ex-husband had been the one holding the gun.

Now, Jane is demanding that Texan police reopen the investigation into her only child’s death – saying the force that investigated the shooting failed to pick up on key facts of the case.

Police records show that the shooting had been ruled an accident within a day of the investigation being launched, the inquest into Lucy’s death heard.



American cops are understood to have opened an investigation into a potential charge of “criminally negligent homicide” but Lucy’s dad was never charged over the killing.

A grand jury of 12 civilians was asked to consider if Kris should face a criminal trial in June last year.

The Collin County jury reviewed the case but no charges were subsequently brought against Kris who is reportedly still living in the home where his daughter died.



Kris and Jane split when Lucy was just 4 years old with her dad moving to Saudi Arabia for work and later to Texas.

Kris remarried in the US and had two daughters with his second wife – who Lucy was close with.

Lucy and her dad would remain in touch and he flew to the UK in 2024 for her graduation from Manchester Metropolitan University.

The 23-year-old visited her dad and her half sisters most summers and Christmases.

Lucy’s best friend told the inquest into her death that Lucy had been concerned about her half sisters staying in the same home as her dad’s gun, a 9mm semi-automatic pistol.



Kris is understood to have purchased the firearm from a colleague and kept it at home without a licence or training, which is legal in Texas.

On the morning of her death, Lucy’s boyfriend told the inquest that Kris and his daughter had been arguing.

The pair had got into a disagreement about sexual assault allegations against Donald Trump, who was due to start his second stint as president at the time, the inquest heard.

Lucy reportedly asked her dad “how would you feel if I was the girl in that situation and I’d been sexually assaulted?”

Kris is understood to have responded that he had two other daughters so it wouldn’t bother him much, a comment that upset Lucy.



Later that morning he invited his daughter into his bedroom to see the gun he owned, according to Lucy’s boyfriend.

It was at that point that there was a loud bang and Kris shouted out for his wife.

Rushing into the room, Kris’s second wife and Lucy’s boyfriend found the 23-year-old lying on the floor struggling to breath.



Emergency services were scrambled to the scene but were initially not told that Lucy had been shot, instead hearing that she had passed out, despite the best efforts of medics, Lucy could not be saved.

Police evidence from initial interviews with Kris indicated that he had been drinking that morning but he was never breathalysed at the scene and his blood alcohol content wasn’t tested.

Bodyworn camera footage from responding officers showed Kris later in the day, stood with arms folded, claiming Lucy had asked to see his gun, adding: “I got it out and it just went off.”

Lucy’s family say she already knew he had a firearm and would never have wanted to see it.

Now, Lucy’s mum hopes that the UK coroner’s findings coupled with political pressure from the UK will prompt police to consider launching a fresh investigation into her daughter’s death



Lucy’s mum, who finds it “very hard to feel anything” about her ex-husband, told The Times: “The person I want in my mind and in my heart is Lucy. She cared about the truth.

“Those who really know Lucy’s heart can hear her loud and clear and know exactly what she would want us to do … Make change, do better, be better.”

Even though both Lucy and Harrison hold British citizenship, Cheshire police had no jurisdiction to investigate Lucy’s death.

RECOMMENDED STORIES

The Foreign Office also did not intervene in the US police investigation, telling Lucy’s mum that they could only get involved if there was a “high level of complexity.”

While Jane believes her ex-husband did not intend to shoot Lucy, she does feel he acted recklessly after drinking and raised concerns over contradictions in his story about what happened that morning.

News.title: Yoga teacher ‘cut from husband’s £5m fortune over fears she’d flee UK with their kids’ battles brother-in-law in court
News.content:
WILL AXE Yoga teacher ‘cut from husband’s £5m fortune over fears she’d flee UK with their kids’ battles brother-in-law in court

A YOGA teacher cut from her husband’s £5million fortune over fears she would flee Britain with their kids is now locked in a court battle with her brother-in-law.

Brazil-born Gabriela Teixeira, 41, married wealthy London property investor and restaurateur Abbas Moaven in 2002.





They went on to have two children together and lived in lavish homes in London’s most exclusive areas, including Holland Park and Kensington.

Following Abbas’ death in 2021, Gabriela discovered his estate, which she was due to split with her two adult children, could be worth nothing.

Just weeks before he died, the tycoon had signed documents declaring four properties were not entirely his but shared with his mum and brother, Amir.

She is now fighting Amir in court along with her two children Elis and Aryan to restore the homes to her husband’s estate.

Gabriela claims the documents were a “sham” and has highlighted concerns Amir raised with Abbas that she could leave the UK with the kids.

Her barrister, Alexander Learmonth KC, claims the lawyers’ notes confirm the move was “entered into with the clear aim of defeating a claim by Gabriela and/or her children against the estate.”

He said the properties should fall into Abbas’ estate – leaving Gabriela and the children with up to £5million to inherit after debts are paid.



But Amir insists the declarations of trust were legitimate and were merely putting into writing what had always been the case – that the properties were owned in equal shares by the brothers and their mum.

He also said Gabriela’s allegations amount to an unprovable accusation that her husband lied about not being sole owner of the properties in order to “defraud and/or control her” from beyond the grave.

The court heard Abbas and Amir moved to the UK from Iran in 1982 to live with their dad and later began a West London clothing shop called Homeboy together.

They then moved into the mobile phone market in the mid-1990s and later restaurants, while also buying up a series of properties around the capital.

Gabriela and Abbas met at his restaurant The Gate in Notting Hill and began dating, with Gabriela later moving into the flat he shared in Queen’s Gate, Kensington, with Amir.



She works as a doula – a non-clinical professional who provides physical and emotional support to families before, during and after childbirth – and yoga teacher.

Giving evidence, she said Abbas had never given any indication that the flat was not entirely his property.

When he died aged 45 in 2012 after a cancer battle, his last will left his estate to his widow and their two children.

But the estate was “substantially diminished” when Abbas signed the trust documents that declared four properties in his name were actually owned in by himself, Amir and their mother, Nazemi Tehran.

Gabriela’s barrister Mr Learmonth said the result – if the declaration of trusts stand – is that the estate is potentially “insolvent” due to other debts.



But he told the court the documents were “obviously shams” designed by Abbas to prevent his wife or creditors from making a claim to most of his assets after his death.

One of the notes between Abbas’ solicitor and Amir before his death, read: “His concern was what would happen if Abbas passed away and his wife disappeared with the two children to Brazil.

“How could they prevent this, and how could they prevent her from having access to the funds from the property assets?”

A subsequent note following another meeting added: “Amir confirmed his main intention was to secure Abbas’ children’s welfare because he was certain that any assets passed over to Abbas’ wife would be dissipated.”

Mr Learmonth said the notes “establish clearly that, if the declarations of trust were really intended to have any legal effect at all, then they were entered into with the clear aim of defeating a claim by Gabriela and/or her children against the estate.”



Amir claims the four properties were only ever put into Abbas’ name for “cultural reasons” because he was the elder brother.

But Mr Learmonth said the claim was contradicted by other high-end properties – also around Kensington – being held in the younger brother Amir’s sole name.

He continued: “Gabriela says that Abbas never once said or hinted to her that Amir had any interest in any of the properties, or acted in a way which suggested that he did, though they lived in three of them during their marriage.

“Holland Park and Brasenose were both house-hunted by Gabriela and Abbas together, the former as a place for their growing family to live in and the latter as an investment, which they moved into subsequently.

“This evidence is inconsistent with Amir and Nazemi having any involvement, let alone financial involvement, or there being a common intention that they have a beneficial interest.



“It is deeply unsatisfactory that 14 years later, Gabriela and her two children – now grown to from infancy to adulthood – have still not been able to obtain a proper account of Abbas’ estate, let alone to receive their inheritance.”

For Amir, barrister Lydia Pemberton described the row as a “very bitter family dispute”.

But she insisted the declarations were “the written manifestation of the long-standing agreement” that the properties were held by Abbas, Amir and their mother in equal shares.

RECOMMENDED STORIES

The court heard the brothers’ mum died in 2023, with her will leaving half of her estate to Amir and a quarter each to Gabriela and Abbis’ children.

Deputy Master Bowles has reserved his decision on the case until a later date.

News.title: Dubai cops ‘arrest three drone attack survivors who privately shared pics of blitzed high-rise’ after Iranian attack
News.content:
THREE survivors of a drone attack on a Dubai skyscraper have been arrested for privately sharing pictures of the blitz with their loved ones, reports say.

The “traumatised” victims were inside their high-rise apartments near Dubai Creek Harbour when a drone hit the building on Wednesday – sparking a huge fire.





The trio reportedly took photos in the aftermath of the explosion to show relatives what had happened and assure them that they were safe.

It comes as…

But they were later arrested by local police who requested to see their phones, according to campaign group Detained in Dubai.

The CEO of the group Radha Stirling said on X: “Three traumatised survivors of an Iran drone strike were arrested after privately sharing a photo with loved ones confirming they were alive following an explosion on their apartment floor.



“Instead of receiving victim support, they were locked up by Dubai police.”

Stirling called on authorities to take a more considerate approach towards survivors of the near-death experience.

It comes as UAE authorities continue to crackdown on locals filming and sharing videos or pictures of Iranian attacks on the country.

A British tourist is among dozens of people charged under cybercrime laws after allegedly filming Iranian missiles causing carnage in Dubai.



Those arrested are accused of using an information network or information technology tool to broadcast, publish, republish or circulate false news, rumours or provocative propaganda that may incite public opinion or disturb public security.

The 60-year-old was arrested last week – despite assuring police that he’d deleted the footage from his phone.

If found guilty, they could face up to two years in prison, or fines ranging from £4,000 to £40,600, or both.

Foreign nationals will also be hit with enforced deportations.

This comes just two weeks after the UAE Public Prosecution Office warned against publishing or circulating “rumours” and information from unknown sources.



The UAE's cryptic cyber-crime laws The UAE’s cyber-crime laws are among the strictest in the world. People can face fines of up to £57,000, lengthy detention, and travel bans for social media posts that authorities consider harmful to “public order”, “national unity” or the reputation of the state. Detained in Dubai warns that the legislation is “deliberately broad” – and in times of war they become even more dangerous. Almost anything critical of government policy or regional conflict can be interpreted as a crime. In practice, commentary about regional conflict, government policy or security matters can be interpreted as a criminal offence. Sharing advice about what to do and what not to do in the country, the organisation said: “Do not assume your home country’s free speech protections apply in the UAE. They do not. “Do not assume that sharing international news coverage is safe. It may not be. “Do not assume that deleting a post removes liability. It often does not.”

A statement said: “The Public Prosecution warns against publishing or circulating rumours and information from unknown sources through social media platforms or any other technological means, due to the consequences thereof, including the spread of confusion and harm to the security and stability of society.

“The Public Prosecution further emphasises the necessity of obtaining information solely from official and accredited sources, and refraining from being influenced by content that is published or circulated without verification.

“Any person who shares or reposts content from unknown sources shall be subject to legal accountability in accordance with the applicable legislation, even if they are not the original creator of such content.”

Elsewhere in the Gulf, more than 200 people in Qatar have already been charged under similar laws since the conflict began, according to Detained in Dubai.



For years, Dubai has cultivated a reputation as a glamorous and attractive destination – and more than 250,000 British nationals have made it their home in recent years.

But many have fled amid the onslaught of Iranian drones and rockets in the past three weeks.

On Monday, a drone strike on a fuel tank on Dubai International forced the airport to cancel and suspend several flights.

A huge fire sent plumes of smoke billowing above the city’s skyline – marking the third attack on Dubai Airport since the war broke out three weeks ago.



One person also died in Abu Dhabi after a missile landed on a car – with authorities saying the deceased individual was of Palestinian nationality.

Meanwhile, Donald Trump has warned that Nato will face a “very bad” future if it doesn’t help open the Strait of Hormuz.

The US president called on the defence pact to help unblock the key shipping waterway – a passage for one-fifth of the global oil supply.

RECOMMENDED STORIES

Hitting out at the alliance, Trump told the Financial Times: “We didn’t have to help them with Ukraine. Ukraine is thousands of miles away from us.

“But we helped them. Now we’ll see if they help us. Because I’ve long said that we’ll be there for them but they won’t be there for us. And I’m not sure that they’d be there.”

News.title: B&M's 'stunning' £40 coffee table 'looks just like' £154 one from Homebase
News.content:
B&M's 'stunning' £40 coffee table 'looks just like' £154 one from Homebase

Having a stylish statement piece could cost much less if people shop around

View 3 Images The B&M table is more than £110 cheaper than the alternative version (Image: Ian Cooper / North Wales Live)

People looking to upgrade the look of their living rooms with some practical furniture might be interested in B&M's newest coffee table. It looks "just like" a version that is sold elsewhere and costs shoppers over £110 more.

‌



B&M bosses described the Home Collection Coffee Table as having a "stunning light oak finish" and added it was "perfect for resting drinks, books or remotes". The 'stylish' table offers plenty of surface space with a 60cm table that is raised 40cm off the floor.

‌



The most attractive detail is wrapped around the base of the table with the curved wooden slats that go all around the furniture piece. The B&M version looks like a much more expensive version being sold in Homebase stores across the UK.

‌



Shoppers might see some similarities between it and the Florence Yale Oak Coffee Table. It also has a similar wooden slat design around the base and a generous "wild oak" surface.

The biggest difference shoppers will spot straight away is the price. Homebase's version costs £154, down from £171 - a massive £114 difference compared to B&M's £40 option.

View 3 Images B&M is selling the coffee table in stores for £40 (Image: B&M)

‌



Homebase bosses described the "easy-to-assemble" table as being capable of "making a statement". The product description added: "Its textured matt wild oak effect top and elegant pillar base create a contemporary feel, perfect for adding a touch of modern sophistication to your living room.

"The unique lamella design is the finishing touch, ensuring this piece is as versatile as it is stylish." When looking at the assembly details, it confirms that the Homebase version is slightly larger - with an 80cm tabletop adding an extra 20cm compared to B&M's - and a tiny bit taller at 42cm.

For people on a budget or looking to fill a slightly smaller space, the B&M option would be ideal. It would also save homeowners over £110, which could then go towards other home improvements.

‌



View 3 Images The more expensive Homebase version costs over £110 more than B&M's similar design (Image: Homebase)

B&M is known for offering its customers a bargain deal that might look a lot like some more expensive alternatives. Recently, a £5 vase was compared to a pricier pattern also available at John Lewis for £30.

And, sticking with furniture, there was a £25 display unit with wooden slats built in that looked very similar to a popular piece of storage sold by Swedish furniture giant IKEA for £50. The B&M version was half the price and also featured the decorative touch at no extra cost.

Article continues below

Most of the current range is available to view online here, but shoppers can't order it and must try in stores to make a purchase. There are more than 770 B&M stores in the UK as of early 2026 across many towns and cities. Shoppers can find their nearest using the online tool here, which needs a postcode to locate nearby branches.

News.title: TV expert claims Brits have hundreds of millions worth of hidden treasures in sheds
News.content:
TV expert claims Brits have hundreds of millions worth of hidden treasures in sheds

Restoration expert Henry Cole has visited over 1,000 sheds and garages across the UK and believes Brits are sitting on a goldmine of valuable items including vintage collectables and antiques

View 3 Images TV Presenter Henry Cole believes garages and sheds hold million pounds of valuables (Image: BLAZE)

Restoration guru Henry Cole reckons Brits have hundreds of millions of quid's worth of treasures tucked away in their sheds and garages. The telly host, who is best known known for World’s Greatest Motorcycle Rides, has popped into over 1,000 sheds and is convinced we've only just begun to unearth the value of these stashed goods.

‌



He points out that concealed private collections range from hundreds of motors and scores of aircraft to troves of tractors and motorbikes. Cole suggests that bits and bobs like car memorabilia and vintage oil cans and jugs could rake in a tidy sum rather than being chucked out.

‌



View 3 Images Brits should check if they have any automotive related valuables in their sheds (Image: BLAZE)

‌



Branded examples like Shell, Texaco and Pratt from the 1940-50s can put some cash in your pocket with rare oil cans fetching £2,000 or more, whilst exceptional examples can be sold for more than £3,000.

The advice comes after research of 2,000 adults found 65% have things in their sheds they haven't used in years, with an average value of more than £800. It was commissioned by BLAZE, which is launching a new series of Junk & Disorderly, which is on every Monday at 9pm.

Henry Cole, who presents the show, said: "Where Brits go wrong is that a lot of things they're holding onto are worthless, from bikes to mobility scooters to brown furniture and bathroom goods.

‌



"But at the same time, there is millions of millions of pounds worth of automotive related items, and when I go into a shed I can very quickly sift through the rubbish and find the good stuff.

"There are loads of things that can be valuable which don't look valuable, like a G plan and mid-century furniture."

The study found the most common items adults have lying around include garden equipment or tools (65%), DIY materials (58%) and old tools or machinery (43%). But 59% hold onto items as they might be useful one day, whilst 34% said they have sentimental value.

‌



View 3 Images Henry Cole is the host of BLAZE's new series of Junk & Disorderly (Image: BLAZE)

It also emerged 14% would restore old bicycles from their garage or shed if they could and 12% would rebuild or repair tools or machinery they own. Meanwhile, 45% reckon they likely have items of monetary value in their shed or garage that they don't use. And 42% know others who could also be sitting on a small fortune from unused items at home.

However, 48% confessed the only thing that would motivate them to sell or discard their unused items would be if they relocated. A quarter even admitted they have sold or tossed something away that they now believe to be worth a lot of money, and 31% thought it was just junk.

‌



And of those who participated in the study, conducted via OnePoll.com, 53% have possessions they would rather pass down to younger generations than flog them.

A spokesperson for BLAZE and the show Junk & Disorderly, said: "Adults are far more likely to be sitting on hidden treasures than they realise, with sheds and garages often packed with items that could be worth serious money.

"We want to highlight just how easy it is to separate genuine gems from everyday clutter and how satisfying it can be to turn forgotten belongings into cash. It's all about taking a second look before writing something off as junk."

Article continues below

15 ITEMS BRITS HAVE LYING AROUND IN THEIR SHEDS AND GARAGES:

News.title: Putin speaks out on claims Iran's new leader secretly flown for life-saving op
News.content:
Vladimir Putin's spokesman has refused to comment on reports that Iran's new supreme leader may have been secretly flown to Russia for life-saving surgery.

Mojtaba Khamenei, 56, was reportedly critically injured in the Israeli strike on February 28 that killed six of his family members, including his father, Ayatollah Ali Khamenei. Tehran's ambassador to Cyprus confirmed the reports, telling the Guardian last week that: "I have heard that he was injured in his legs and hand and arm … I think he is in the hospital because he is injured."

The Russian president's spokesman Dmitry Peskov failed to confirm or deny claims that Mojtaba Khamenei had been smuggled out of Iran and was receiving treatment in a Russian presidential clinic. "We do not comment on such reports," Peskov said.

The reports surfaced in Kuwait based on "Iranian sources". A senior source close to the new leader reportedly told Kuwait's Al-Jarida newspaper that he had been transported to Moscow on a Russian military aircraft in a "highly secret operation due to his health and security situation".

Image: POOL/AFP via Getty Images) POOL/AFP via Getty Images)

Ensure our latest headlines always appear at the top of your Google Search by making us a Preferred Source. Click here to activate or add us as your Preferred Source in your Google search settings.

Upon arrival, he supposedly underwent a successful surgical operation before receiving further treatment in a hospital inside one of Putin’s presidential palaces. These claims have not been independently verified.

Israel has stated it "knows where he [Mojtaba] is" after US President Donald Trump suggested Iran's new Supreme Leader may already be dead.

"I don't know if he's even alive. So far, nobody's been able to show him," Trump said on Saturday. "I'm hearing he's not alive, and if he is, he should do something very smart for his country, and that's surrender."

Russia is suspected of sharing intelligence with the Iranian regime, assisting in strikes on Western military bases in the Middle East.

Lieutenant General Nick Perry, the chief of joint operations, told Defence Secretary John Healey on Thursday that it appeared Russia had provided tactical advice to Iran and its proxies.

Image: PA) PA)

"We have definitely seen the Iranian tactics of the use of their drones learnt from Russians - flying them much lower so they are more effective," he said, adding that this has "proven problematic" because Shahed drones are becoming one of Tehran’s more effective weapons. "There is no doubt [the Iranian] tactics have changed," he added.

Mr Healey said Putin's "hidden hand" is likely aiding Iran in the war launched by the US and Israel on February 28. "No one will be surprised to believe that Putin's hidden hand is behind some of the Iranian tactics, potentially some of their capabilities as well," he said.

The defence secretary argued this was partly "because the one world leader that is benefiting from sky-high oil prices at the moment is Putin because it helps him with a fresh supply of funds for his brutal war in Ukraine". He added that the Russian president "is clearly likely to welcome this war more than anyone else".

News.title: Meningitis 'new strain' fears after 'very unusual' Kent outbreak kills two students
News.content:
Meningitis 'new strain' fears after 'very unusual' Kent outbreak kills two students

UK Health Security Agency is contacting 30,000 students, teachers and parents in the Kent area to get devastating meningitis outbreak under control

View 2 Images People have been hospitalised after a number of cases were detected

Scientists fear a new strain of meningitis could be responsible for an outbreak which has killed two students.

‌



A Year 13 pupil from Queen Elizabeth's Grammar School in Faversham is the second person to have died following an outbreak of meningitis in the Canterbury area. The other death was of a University of Kent student and the UK Health Security Agency is contacting thousands of them to get checked.

‌



At least 11 other people, mainly students, are said to be seriously ill in hospital. There have been 13 confirmed cases and UKHSA is analysing PCR test results from students affected and close contacts.

‌



Prof Andrew Preston, leading expert from the University of Bath, said: “An outbreak of this size and speed is very unusual and of great concern. It is important to characterise the cause of the outbreak. A sudden change in disease pattern could indicate something like a new strain with different behaviours.”

In a post on Facebook, local MP Helen Whately said: "The meningitis outbreak in our area is a huge shock. Feeling so deeply sad for the young lives lost - a year 13 pupil at QEGS [Queen Elizabeth's Grammar School] and uni of Kent student. My heart goes out to their families.

View 2 Images Meningitis can be deadly

‌



Kent nightclub Club Chemistry breaks silence after horror meningitis outbreak kills 2 READ MORE :

Meningitis' little known sign that appears in the hands and feet READ MORE :

"It's incredibly worrying too for the families of the young people in hospital, and others at risk. I am asking the NHS urgently for more information and guidance, especially given the rumours going round about where they may have picked it up."

Uptake of meningitis vaccines among adolescents is 73% and scientists are now trying to work out whether many of those who fell seriously ill were vaccinated. If so, it could suggest a new strain has started to evade immunity.

Keith Neal, retired Professor of the Epidemiology of Infectious Disease at Nottingham University, said: “Under 5s are most at risk to becoming seriously unwell with meningitis but teenagers and students are at a higher risk due to social mixing. There are two vaccines, MenB which is part of the childhood schedule and ACYW135 which is given to teenagers. Determining the strain will help tell us what vaccine might be useful. There may be a slightly different strain of meningitis which can spread between people.”

‌



Antibiotics are being given to students in Canterbury regarded as "close contacts" of those affected, such as living in the same accommodation block, as a preventative measure after the outbreak was confirmed over the weekend. Meningococcal bacteria can cause serious illness, including inflammation of the lining of the brain and blood poisoning, which can rapidly lead to sepsis. Anyone can get meningitis but it is most common in babies, children, teenagers and young adults and it can spread very quickly in places such as universities and colleges.

Trish Mannes, UKHSA regional deputy director for the South East, said: "We understand that many people at the university and in the wider community will be affected by this sad news and we would like to offer our condolences to the friends and family involved. Students and staff will understandably be feeling worried about the risk of further cases; however, we would like to reassure them that close contacts of cases have been given antibiotics as a precautionary measure.

"Advice and support is being offered to the wider student community, and to local hospitals and NHS 111, and we're monitoring the situation closely. Meningococcal disease can progress rapidly, so it's essential that students and staff are alert to the signs and symptoms of meningococcal meningitis and septicaemia, which can include a fever, headache, rapid breathing, drowsiness, shivering, vomiting and cold hands and feet.

Article continues below

"Septicaemia can also cause a characteristic rash that does not fade when pressed against a glass. Students are particularly at risk of missing the early warning signs of meningitis because they can be easily confused with other illnesses such as a bad cold, flu or even a hangover. That's why it's vital that, if a friend goes to bed unwell, you check on them regularly and don't hesitate to seek medical help by contacting their GP or calling NHS 111 if they have these symptoms or you're concerned about them. This could save their life."

A University of Kent spokesperson said: "The safety of our students and staff remains our highest priority. We are working closely with public health teams and are in touch with staff and students to ensure they get the advice and support they need."

News.title: Taiwan under threat from Chinese planes ahead of major Donald Trump visit
News.content:
Taiwan under threat from Chinese planes ahead of major Donald Trump visit

China's surge of military activity around Taiwan comes just days before Donald Trump is set to visit Beijing - and adds to fears that an invasion could be coming in the next 12 months

View 3 Images Chinese fighter jets have been spotted around Taiwan (Image: AP)

China has been flying fighter planes perilously close to Taiwan just days before a major visit by Donald Trump, officials on the island have warned.

‌



The Taiwanese defence ministry reported a surge of Chinese military planes near the island after noticing an unexplained drop in flights over the past two weeks, sparking puzzled conversations among top brass.

‌



A total of 25 jets were identified on Saturday, with 16 of them entering its northern, central and southwestern Air Defence Identification Zone. Seven naval ships were also spotted around the island around the island.

‌



The increased number of aircraft after an unexplained drop-off in activity comes as speculation mounts over whether Beijing will use spiralling conflict around the world as a cover for its long-planned invasion of the island.

View 3 Images Xi Jinping has previously told defence chiefs to prepare for an invasion of Taiwan next year, US intelligence says (Image: AP)

Keir Starmer LIVE: PM will announce major energy bills help in Iran war press conference READ MORE :

Chinese retail giant launches in UK today to rival Amazon READ MORE :

While these flights have fallen in the past during major events and public holidays - with the past two weeks coinciding with the annual meeting of the Chinese Communist Party - this year’s fall was more prominent than in the past, experts said.

‌



It comes ahead of a visit by US President Donald Trump. The White House has said that Trump would travel to China from March 31 to April 2, though Beijing has not officially confirmed the dates.

Some observers also suggested the decline may be driven by a shift to a next phase in China’s military training and modernisation, with the army appearing to be exploring a new model for joint training between its forces.

View 3 Images Taiwan has been carrying out military drills to prepare for a Chinese invasion (Image: AFP/Getty Images)

‌



China has repeatedly vowed to seize Taiwan by force if necessary, with warplanes and navy vessels spotted heading towards the island on a near-daily basis.

US intelligence has previously warned that 2027 has been singled out as the likely date of a future invasion, with Xi Jinping reported ordered the People's Liberation Army (PLA) to be capable of seizing the island by the People's Liberation Army's 100th anniversary in 2027.

Taiwan has been stepping up its defence as it prepares for an invasion next year as a likely scenario, including detailed military drills and mandatory conscription programme.

‌



Defence Minister Wellington Koo said that China’s navy has remained active in nearby waters, even as military flights have fallen off.

China and Taiwan have been governed separately since 1949, when the Communist Party rose to power in Beijing following a bloody civil war.

Defeated Nationalist Party forces fled to Taiwan, which later transitioned from martial law to a democracy.

Article continues below

Beijing has long claimed the government in Taiwan is illegitimate and has vowed to "reunify" with its neighbour, which it claims as part of its own territory

News.title: Meningitis outbreak LIVE: Kent horror queues for antibiotics as two students die
News.content:
A university student and a sixth-form pupil have died after a deadly meningitis outbreak.

The 'devastating' outbreak in Kent is thought to be connected to a recent social gathering - with 11 others still seriously ill.

The two deceased have not been named, but are believed to be aged between 17 and 21. The majority of those hospitalised as a result of the outbreak are within the same age range, and are also believed to be students.

More than 30,000 students and staff have been alerted by the UK Health Security Agency (UKHSA), and have been told to be vigilant against symptoms.

News.title: Royal Mail warns 63 UK postcodes they won't receive mail on time – full list
News.content:
Royal Mail warns 63 UK postcodes they won't receive mail on time – full list

Areas across England, Wales and Scotland are impacted by the delays.

View 2 Images Royal Mail has issued a delivery update (Image: inyourArea)

Postcodes across the UK have been hit by Royal Mail delays. The postal service has shared a list of areas that are currently facing disruption. It means homes and businesses in those areas won't receive their mail on time.

‌



Royal Mail aims to deliver letters and parcels six days a week. However, it has faced multiple disruptions across the UK. As of Monday, March 16, Royal Mail's air and road network is said to have operated to schedule over the past 24 hours.

‌



However, Birmingham Mail Centre has reported that not all of its mail was processed and despatched to schedule over the weekend.

‌



In particular, mail posted on Friday, in the B, HR and WR postcode areas, due to be delivered on Saturday, may not have arrived on time.

Royal Mail said: "We aim to deliver to all addresses we have mail for, six days a week. In a small number of local offices, this may temporarily not be possible due to local issues such as high levels of sick absence, resourcing, or other local factors.

View 2 Images Multiple areas across the UK are affected (Image: inyourArea)

‌



"In those cases, we will rotate deliveries to minimise the delay to individual customers. We also provide targeted support to those offices to address their challenges and restore our service to the high standard our customers would normally receive.

"We're sorry for any inconvenience and thank you for your understanding."

Currently, Royal Mail is reporting delays across 20 depots. These are from all over the UK, including Glasgow, Bristol, Pontyclun, Sunderland and Swindon.

‌



It has had a knock-on effect on 63 postcode areas, meaning people in those parts of England, Wales and Scotland won't get their mail on time.

The delays come as Royal Mail is set to increase the price of stamps next month.

Postcodes facing delays today

Bristol East DO (BS5)
